phenomenon: Sagittarius A* — Milky Way Galactic Center Black Hole
key_measurement: Mass: 4.297 ± 0.012 × 10⁶ solar masses (4.297 million M☉); Distance: 26,996 ± 33 light-years (8,277 ± 9 parsecs); Shadow ring angular size: 51.8 ± 2.3 microarcseconds (EHT Collaboration 2022, ApJL 930 L12; GRAVITY Collaboration 2022)
principle_set: General relativity predicts that the photon capture cross-section of a black hole forms an observable 'shadow' — for Sgr A*, this region subtends ~52 microarcseconds, making it the largest apparent angular size of any black hole as seen from Earth despite its relatively modest mass

Stellar orbital tracking of the S-star cluster — particularly the star S2, which completes a full orbit in 16 years — provides an independent precise measurement of Sgr A*'s mass and the Earth-to-galactic-center distance

Sgr A*'s accretion flow is highly variable on timescales of minutes to hours (compared to days for M87*), because its smaller mass means orbital timescales near the event horizon are far shorter, complicating EHT imaging
Radio interferometry at 230 GHz (1.3 mm wavelength) can penetrate the interstellar scattering screen that blocks optical and ultraviolet observations of the galactic center

The observed ring morphology is consistent with a Kerr (rotating) black hole in general relativity; alternative compact object models (boson stars, gravastars) are disfavored by the observed shadow size and shape

Sgr A* is currently in an extremely low-luminosity accretion state — about 10⁹ times less luminous than a typical active galactic nucleus of comparable mass — making it an unusually quiet supermassive black hole
framework: General relativity; Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI); Keplerian orbital mechanics of S-stars; Near-infrared interferometry (GRAVITY/VLTI)
institutions: Max Planck Institute for Extraterrestrial Physics (MPE) — Reinhard Genzel group
UCLA Galactic Center Group — Andrea Ghez group
GRAVITY Collaboration / ESO Very Large Telescope Interferometer

sources.claim: EHT released first image of Sagittarius A* on May 12, 2022, showing a ring-like structure with angular size 51.8 ± 2.3 microarcseconds consistent with the predicted black hole shadow
sources.citation: Event Horizon Telescope Collaboration 2022, ApJL 930 L12 (Paper I: Introduction and Summary)

sources.claim: Mass of Sgr A* measured at 4.297 ± 0.012 × 10⁶ solar masses and distance 8,277 ± 9 pc (26,996 ly) from GRAVITY interferometer tracking S2 stellar orbit at the VLT
Sgr A* discovered as a compact radio source in 1974 by Balick and Brown using NRAO baseline interferometer; the asterisk designation added 1982 by Brown to denote 'excited' nature of the source
Reinhard Genzel and Andrea Ghez awarded the 2020 Nobel Prize in Physics for discovering that Sgr A* is a supermassive compact object — the best empirical evidence that supermassive black holes exist
sources.citation: Nobel Committee for Physics 2020, Nobel Prize in Physics announcement

sources.claim: Star S2 reached closest approach (periapsis) to Sgr A* in 2018 at ~120 AU, moving at 2.55% the speed of light (7,650 km/s), with gravitational redshift observed consistent with general relativity

visualization_notes: The EHT image of Sgr A* shows a glowing orange-gold ring of emission — similar in visual character to M87* but notably fuzzier in the released image due to rapid variability during the observation. The ring is roughly symmetric (unlike M87*'s asymmetric crescent), consistent with a nearly face-on accretion disk orientation. Shadow diameter: ~52 microarcseconds — coincidentally almost identical apparent size to M87* in the sky, despite Sgr A* being ~1,500x less massive and ~2,000x closer. The physical shadow diameter is ~51.8 million km (~0.35 AU), smaller than Mercury's orbit. The Schwarzschild radius is 0.08 AU (12 million km). For S-star visualization: the S2 star traces a tight Keplerian ellipse around an invisible point (the black hole) with a 16-year period — a powerful 'proving' visual. The galactic center is hidden behind ~25 magnitudes of interstellar dust extinction in visible light — only radio, infrared, and X-ray can see Sgr A*. Shot ideas: galaxy overhead view showing Earth-to-center distance of 26,000 light-years; S2 orbital animation; zoom to 52-microarcsecond shadow ring; comparison frame with M87* at same angular scale.
visualization_compliance: EHT images of Sgr A* released open access — credit 'EHT Collaboration' required for direct image use

The image was produced from 2017 observation data but released May 12, 2022 after five years of processing — the delay is scientifically significant (Sgr A* variability required novel imaging methods)
The shadow is an image of the photon ring region, not the singularity — do not depict or narrate about the interior of the black hole as observable
Hawking radiation from Sgr A* is theoretical and unmeasurably small — do not depict as observed
The Galactic Center is invisible in optical wavelengths due to dust — optical imagery of 'the galactic center' in documentaries is always artist impression unless clearly labelled as infrared/radio
Nobel Prize attribution: Reinhard Genzel (MPE) and Andrea Ghez (UCLA) shared the 2020 Nobel for the S-star work; Roger Penrose received the other half for GR black hole formation theorem
